sentences.text: So I was received there.

And they told me that they were asked by the Gestapo If they would submit to them letters of permission from any kind of Jewish person to go to the Baltic States for [NON-ENGLISH], which means preparation to work in Israel.
They would let them out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE]
So we had typewriters.
And we typed out-- we had letterheads from the Baltic States.
[INAUDIBLE] I still have a few in my portfolio.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we wrote down so-and-so, born so-and-so, is admitted-- has been admitted to [NON-ENGLISH] for six months, signed so-and-so, signed our organization.
The president signed it.
It was signed.
You asked me how we got the names.
We got the names from friends of ours from the organization.
And every friend has a friend, so we got from them and from other people, from whom we knew.
And so it was especially-- most of them were young people at that time, in their teens or 20s.
And we sent this out and these people came out.
And by the evening time, I went back to Bremen.
I think about 200 people.
Only through the-- through Parchman, 'park-men.' I helped him, because I-- I knew some people name were given to me from the congregation.
And I talked to them to let them out.
And he sometimes let them out.
Sometimes he didn't.
You had to-- he very moody.
At certain times he did and certain times he did not.
Got them out this way.
I think he wasn't very intelligent.
I mean, he was very smart, but not very learned in any kind of especial-- in a special way, you see?
He was a tough guy.
And like the Nazis wanted, the less you know, the more you could impress them with their philosophy and they would believe everything, you see.
He didn't have a mind of its own.
That came in different way.
As I mentioned before, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after Kristallnacht, we wrote to all these friends again, all over.
And now it became actually serious, dangerous to stay in Germany.
So when this cousin of my father's who was living in Saskatchewan-- Yorkton, Saskatchewan, and he was a farmer.
And since in-- in Canada they have a parliament-- at that time they had a parliament.
And they have congresspeople they call an MP, Member of Parliament, MO.
They had a member of parliament.
And in these small places like he was, he was very close to this friend.
And this friend, you see, was able that they made a special law for us to come in.
They sent us then what we called a landing card.
We are allowed to land in Canada.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] However, even with the landing card, it wasn't easy, because now we had to have money to go by boat.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Germans wanted at that time that all the Jewish people should leave the money in Germany as much as possible.
And not only this.
If they would travel outside, they should lose-- use a German shipping company-- there were two Germany shipping companies-- and not [NON-ENGLISH] from Hamburg.
My father went there and they told him, we have no vacancy, no place 'til two years from now.
And we didn't want to wait two years, of course, because things were getting worse every day.
So he went to Hamburg.
Hamburg was the biggest city.
Hamburg had two million people and Bremen only 360,000.
So he went to Hamburg.
And in Hamburg at that time, 1939, that was still before the war-- so he went to an English company, British company, the Cunard-White Star Line, C-U-N-I-- C-U-N-A-R-D, Cunard-White, you know.
[LAUGHS]
And there he met in person, a British person.
And he said he will make arrangements for us.
He will make arrangements that we will leave by ferry from Hamburg to England.
And from Southampton, we would go to Canada.
However, I did not tell you in order to get tickets, you need money.
And to get this money was also a problem, because we had to sell our house.
Now, the Nazi planted a woman into our house next door, [INAUDIBLE] into our house.
She was living there.
Don't remember.
And she was willing to buy the house, but of course was very little money.
It was just enough money, maybe 1,200 marks or something like this, to get the tickets.
So we had the money for the tickets, but that's all.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So finally, we got this arrangement with this agent in Hamburg, with the Cunard-White Star Line.
And now we had to prepare to move out.
Before you move out, you had to tell the Nazis when you pack and where you pack.
And someone would be there to watch that you wouldn't take anything along.
They were always afraid of themselves, of their own lives.
And they didn't want to be afraid of other [INAUDIBLE] life.
[LAUGHS] So they-- you would be a spy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So they had to stay there.
And when we packed-- and people packed two kinds of things.
They packed-- one is a van.
A van is a big box, you see, which is shipped-- shipped separately by-- in advance.
And then you take a little suitcase with you.
When they packed the van, they were standing there that you did not move anything in.
If you moved-- if you put anything in without them knowing, you see, you might go to concentration camp and wouldn't be-- be let out at all.
I was always-- I always wanted to put in a few items, newspapers and so on.
Only one item I was [INAUDIBLE]-- I managed to put in a newspaper.
I packed-- wrapped something else in it, which had the list of the names.
Jews had to have certain names at that time.
Any name which was Jewish had to add-- which according to them was not Jewish.
Joseph is a real Jewish name, but according to them it was not a Jewish name, because Joseph Goebbels.
So I had to change my name-- not change my name.
They put it in my-- on my certificates.
Israel, Jacob Israel, Gert Israel.
My sister's name was Elise Sarah.
Girls had to take the name Sarah.
So I had this-- I have this with me, you see, names which they considered Jewish.
They made fun of names anyhow.
So that was all supposed to be arranged.
Besides this, it was to be arranged that we get [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] where you sign out.
So before we left we had to sign out.
So I had to go again to the police station.
I did this because my father spoke mainly Russian, but he knew a little bit German too.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so the man that's sitting there, the official that was sitting there, said to me, write it out.
Fill it out.
Fill it out.
You didn't sign in where are you going.
I filled it in.
I said, we are going to Canada.
You didn't fill the street and the city.
I said, I don't know it yet.
He said, OK, never mind, because we Nazis are going to conquer the world and we find you wherever you go.
[LAUGHS] So-- so I said, OK, I hope not.
So we went on.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I forgot to tell you that one night-- the Nazis were always afraid that other people would find out what they are doing.
So one night, they came and they knocked in the middle of the night.
They knocked at our house.
And my father and I were sleeping in the house in Bremen.
And I looked out of the window.
I saw a man standing on the other side.
Of course, he was not in Nazi uniform, you see.
That would be too obvious.
He was in plain civilian clothing.
And I didn't go down.
Then I said to him, I said, wait.
He knocked and knocked again.
After one hour or so, I said to myself, what can you lose?
Go down.
I went down.
And down, he said, here I have a paper to look through to make a search, search warrant, to look through your house.
Said, go and look.
Here, over, he turned this over, turned this over.
[LAUGHS] At the end I said to him, what are you looking for?
We're looking for your brother.
I said to him, what?
And you don't know that?
He's no longer in-- in Sachsenhausen.
He went to the hospital at Nuremberg.
Didn't you know that?
He said, OK.
I'm sorry.
Goodbye.
But that's what kind of scaring events you have, ominous nights.
I knew already 1933 they had certain concentration camps, because we had a non-Jewish friend who was a doll doctor, he fixed dolls.
And he-- but I don't know if he was a Communist or not.
But anyhow, he was sent to concentration camp.
I don't know which the first one was, if it was Dachau or it was the other one, Buchenwald, one of these was the first.
Sachsenhausen was formed in 1936, when they had the Olympics.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, his wife gets an urn with ashes in and said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was shot while he tried to escape.
These are his ashes, already 1933.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I knew that there were some.
And also from other people I heard at that time.
They kept it all very quiet.
But I knew already at that time there were concentration camps and there were prisons and they were shooting and killing already at that time.
When you left Germany-- when did you actually leave Germany?
We left Germany on March-- on May 31st, 1939.
And on that day, we went from Bremen to Hamburg by train.
And we saw our relatives for the last time.
And we took a taxi from our relatives to the harbor, to the port.
And at that time, we took a taxi.
And the taxi had to make a detour.
And I found out that the detour was because Goering, who was one of the Nazi officials, gave a talk.
And that was the first time where he talked openly, telling the German people that we are sending soldiers to Franco's Spain.
We knew this all the time, but he never wanted to acknowledge it.
When we came to the port, was a small ship, 1,400 tons-- pretty ship-- and a ferry boat.
And we were all searched.
My father was searched more than we were.
And we went down there and we went out and we left Germany.
Now, we went first on the Elbe.
Hamburg is also a little bit inside the river Elbe.
And then we come to a point where the Elbe flows into the North Sea, the German Sea they call it.
Cuxhaven is the name of that place.
And then we went a little bit further.
There is an island, Heligoland, which was bombed during the war and submerged, and afterwards Germany brought it up again.
Up to that point, everything went fine and well.
But then afterwards, I got very seasick.
[LAUGHS] And and we came.
We went on and we traveled-- instead of traveling 36 hours-- for the 24 hours it was 36 hours.
And we landed then finally Friday morning.
I think we left Wednesday evening.
We landed at-- on Friday morning 9:00 or 10 o'clock in a little village in England called Grimsby, G-R-I-M-S-B-Y.
That's a bay in the center of Britain.
And we only were allowed to take $4 out.
Each one was only allowed $4.
And that was signed in the book, in the-- in the passport and all over, $4 no more.
And $4 at that time was 10 Reichsmark, 10 mark.
At that time the mark was $2.50.
Don't forget, $1 was $2.50.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I took-- I think I took a haircut.
From there, we were received by a man from-- by a company, Cunard-White Star.
And they bought us by train, very quick train, from Grimsby to London.
And in London, they had a bus.
They showed us around London.
We hadn't seen London never.
And they brought us to a hotel at East End in London.

And that was for the first time since 1933 that we had good food again and meat for the first time, because when Hitler came to power, one of the first things, besides other things, was to forbade-- he forbade [NON-ENGLISH], which means kosher slaughtering.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to-- we came to London.
And we stayed in London over Shabbat 'til Sunday.
We went to the big synagogue which was nearby.
There was a chief rabbi.
The synagogue was later burned out.
And we went over the Tower Bridge.
We saw that sort of thing.
Next day, on Sunday, we went to Southampton.
And at 3 o'clock afternoon, we left Southampton in a boat by name of Alaunia, A-L-A-U-N-I-A, which was a 14,000-ton book-- boat, 10 times as big as the other.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so we left Germany.
It was my father, my sister, and my two brothers, five people.
First of all, our trip was a very eventful trip, I must say.
[LAUGHS]
Because usually in summer, the boats go a little bit higher, because on the-- on the globe, you see, that's a shorter way to Canada.
And we went too far up and we came into the section of the icebergs.
And our boat had to stay-- stand still for about two or three days.
We heard the foghorns all the time.
We couldn't go on deck.
Everything was closed.
We saw fish, flying fish coming over the deck.
And we-- we were actually standing in water until the fog subsided and we continued.
Then we came to Labrador, to Newfoundland, and we came into the St.
Lawrence River in Canada.
Now, when we came to Quebec, we made a little stop.
And a man came to our boat from the Jewish immigrant Aid Society.
And he said to us, I'm supposed to talk to all the refugees-- refugees.
We were refugees.
And I have to tell them that the Canadian government told us to bring you as quickly as possible to your destination, which is Yorkton, Saskatchewan.
So when you come to Montreal, I'll be there.
And I will arrange it for you so that you leave tomorrow.
Well, we arrived in Montreal.
It was a very nice trip, very beautiful trip on the St.
Lawrence River from Quebec to Montreal.
We came to Montreal.
It's a big city.
And we came there after 9 o'clock in the evening, Thursday evening.
Now, the boat company has a-- has a regulation that if people come there after 9 o'clock in the evening, they can either go out or they can stay on the boat 'til next morning.
So we had no one to await us.
The other refugees on the boat had family waiting for them and so on.
We had no one there, so we stayed-- we stayed 'til the next morning.
We stayed 'til the next morning and then we went out.
Then the man came and he took us out.
He said, I bring you to a little restaurant.
And then I bring you to the train station.
And from there, the train will leave at 12 o'clock to Saskatchewan.
It's a five-day trip, because the Canadian railroad at that time, you see, had mainly freight trains, and they stop at every little village.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we didn't want to go there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So next day, when we came to this railway station, I remember it was like a shack and It was so hot.
We arrived there on June 15th.
That means instead June 10th, we came June 15th, 1939.
It was so hot.
We were inside.
They had no air conditioning at that time.
So-- so we waited there.

We said-- my father said, I would like to stay here in Montreal and not to go there, because about that time, we received a telegram from-- I don't know how it got there, maybe the HIAS got it, because the HIAS sent a telegram to our relatives and said, you have relatives are here.
They will come to you.
They sent them back a telegram and said, don't come here, because we have a drought.
We haven't had very much farming, not much business.
And so it's better for you to-- see, and then Montreal is a bigger city.
You will find something.
Yorkton a small place, only farms.
So we couldn't go there, actually.
But the government, it was decided we should go, because they wanted everyone to move back, you see, like you see that some Germans who came to Canada were interned.
So what happened is my father said, we have to find out about relatives in Montreal.
I know we have relatives in Montreal, he said, because we had written-- my mother had written to a relative of my father's by name of Kaplan, K-A-P-L-A-N.
And the letter had come back, 'not found.' So we waited a little while.
After a little while, two nuns came in to this place.
And they put on a light and the light said Travelers Aid, Travelers Aid society.
So we went over to the Travelers Aid society and my father went over.
And he wanted to talk to them, but they didn't speak English.
They spoke only French, because in Canada, in Quebec, [LAUGHS] we speak French.
We didn't speak French.
So finally, they found-- he found out that one of the nuns spoke Russian, so my father spoke Russian to her.
And we tell them that we know we have-- we have a relative here and we want to find-- they would certainly keep us here if they would know we are here.
Now, the refugees in Canada were called greenhorns.
I don't know why greenhorns.
They had no green money, no dollar bills.
[LAUGHS] So greenhorns.
So-- so this nun called up-- the telephone book, the address book, and called up and find out the Caplan with a C and Kaplan with a K and who knows what?
Finally, after so many calls, a young person answered the phone and said, I don't know if my mother or father comes from Ekaterinoslav.
That's where my father said he came from in Russia.
And-- but he is a tailor, because that's what we told her.
Comes from-- and he's a tailor.
So he called his mother too, her mother.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Stay here and we come right over.
I have a son who's a pharmacist.
He come over.
He had a little car and he will bring you to us.
So that's how we stayed in Montreal.
I don't know what happened to the tickets.
How long did you stay in Montreal?
My family stayed there all during the war, but not I.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My family, in the meantime, went to see this sponsor of ours in Yorkton.
I never saw him.
We recently made it a tour to Vancouver, where he is living, but I didn't know he was leaving there.
Next year my son found out that he was there.
And he came to visit us last year, so I saw him now.
Now he's 72 years old, you see.
At that time, he was 18 or 19.
[LAUGHS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] Shh.
You said your family stayed in Montreal.
I-- like this.
I stayed in Montreal.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father found another relative there.
His name was Velve Pastionek.
And he told me, I know that you have been in Wurzburg in Germany studying there.
And I found a Jewish paper by name of And there's a picture in front of students who were working-- who had been studying in Wurzburg.
Come over to me.
I will show it to you.
I didn't have in mind to go over and show it to him.
But then I said, what can I lose?
I went over to him.
I showed him the pictures.
He showed me the pictures.
When I saw the picture, I recognized two or three people there.
He said to me, there isn't much you can do in Canada.
But United States is a land of possibilities.
Like they always said, you go there with a shovel, you shovel gold.
[LAUGHS] That's what I thought at that time, you see.
Go to United States.
So at that time, until then it was very easy as a student or so to go to the United States.
But then suddenly in-- they made a law that students need also a visa.
So-- so they sent me right away.
I wrote to this person in-- whom I found on the picture, [INAUDIBLE] is his name.
And he sent me right away an affidavit on the school and said, you have been accepted.
Come here.
But that wasn't enough.
I needed a visa or a passport, I hadn't-- had neither.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So he said to me or they said to me, go to the American consul or go to the Canadian consul in Montreal and talk to them.
So I went to the British consulate first, because Canada was a dominion at that time.
And I went there.
I told them the whole story.
And he said-- I said to him, I came to Canada legally, you see, with a [INAUDIBLE].
So he said to me, you have no passport.
But the Hebrew-- the Jewish agency can give you an affidavit in your passport and I make it to extend it.
Your passport is only good 'til April.
And according to the law, it expires two months before you have to apply for a new one.
That you have to do as soon as you come to Montreal.
So finally, making a long story short, I got permission to go to the United States [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from-- from-- from September 'til February.
I got permission to go to the United States.
Now the next question was, how do I get money to get to the United States?
[LAUGHS] So-- so the Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society gave me $30 and I went by Greyhound bus.
I went by Greyhound bus.
I left on the 7th-- or the 6th or 7th of September.
Next day, Canada declared war, because the war broke out in the meantime.
On September 1st, the war broke out, and Canada entered the war in-- England on the 3rd, and Canada on the 7th.
And I came there.
I went by Greyhound Bus.
In the middle of the night I arrived in New York.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You want to hear that?
Well, what I'd like-- was New York your-- your final destination?
No.
That was not my final destination.
But in 1939, there was a World's Fair in New York.
So my father had a relative whom I called up.
I called him up in the middle of the night.
I didn't know that people sleep at nighttime.
So what happened is [LAUGHS] no one answered the phone, so I waited 'til the morning.
And at that time, they didn't have a telephone in each apartment.
There was one telephone for the whole house.
And they had to call the person down to come to the phone.
So this person came to the phone and he said to me, oh, I'm so glad that you are here.
I come and see you.
Where are you?
The Greyhound station was near Madison Square Garden at that time.
And he said, I come to see you, but it takes two hours.
I didn't know such long distances like in New York, two hours from there to there.
But I waited for him.
He came.
And making a long story short, we went to a restaurant and so forth.
And he showed me the World's Fair.
And then I continued.
I stayed with him one or two days and continued to Baltimore.
And I came to Baltimore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Baltimore, they were very happy I came there.
And I also took a taxi.
Had no money, they paid for this.
And I went to the yeshiva there.
I know the consul with whom I talked was very much interested.
The American consul and the Canadian consul, they were very much interested, and people too.
But I think they didn't want to hear much of the war, because they were afraid in America they might have to enter the war.
At that time, there was this kind of sentiment, you see, don't mix in, the Monroe Doctrine.
